speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're focusing on one of the most practical GitHub skills you can learn early: working with issues. Issues are where questions, bugs, chores, ideas, and accessibility concerns become visible work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is not just paperwork.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. In open source, an issue is often the starting point for collaboration. It helps people notice a problem, understand it, discuss it, and decide what happens next.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before we get into the challenge itself, a few important setup notes. This lesson assumes GitHub's newer issues experience, which has better landmark structure and live announcements for screen reader users. If your account shows a Feature Preview setting for the new issues experience, you can check whether it's enabled from the user menu. If the setting is already pressed or says disable, you're set. If the setting doesn't appear at all, it may already be standard for your account.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that matters because the page structure and announcements can differ a little depending on the interface.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And for NVDA users, remember the browse mode and focus mode rhythm. Browse mode is usually best for reading headings, lists, and issue content. Focus mode is what you want when typing in fields like the issue title, body, or search box. And NVDA plus F7 remains a powerful orientation tool for pulling up headings, links, buttons, landmarks, and form fields.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So even before creating an issue, we're thinking about access, structure, and orientation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Always.

speaker: ALEX
text: This chapter recommends two core activities and one optional extension. First, create your first issue. Second, leave a comment and use an @mention. And optionally, break a larger issue into smaller parts with a sub-issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And this is all issue work, not branch work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. No branch is required here. No file editing is required for the main tasks. The point is to become comfortable in issue threads and issue workflows. Your evidence is your issue activity itself: the issue you filed, the comment you posted, and optionally the sub-issue you created.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also a nice learning pattern here: claim, act, confirm.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Claim the task, do the action, then verify from what GitHub shows you.

speaker: ALEX
text: If you prefer the terminal, keep a simple set of principles in mind. First, confirm which repository you're working in. Commands like checking remotes or viewing the repository help you avoid acting in the wrong place. Second, read before you write. View an issue before commenting or editing it. Third, keep comments focused and actionable. Fourth, verify each action from the output. And fifth, stick to documented command options from help and the manual.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the terminal isn't just about speed. It's also about disciplined checking.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And several issue tasks can be done from the GitHub CLI: listing your assigned challenges, viewing a specific issue in the terminal, leaving a comment, and even creating a new issue interactively.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's ground this. What is a GitHub issue, in plain language?

speaker: ALEX
text: A GitHub issue is a trackable conversation about work. It can represent a bug, a feature idea, missing documentation, an accessibility problem, a task for maintainers, or a question to resolve. It has a title, a body, comments, metadata like labels and assignees, and a status such as open or closed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's lighter than a pull request, but more structured than a chat message.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfectly put.

speaker: ALEX
text: To find issues in a repository, you can go through the repository page and activate the Issues tab, or go directly to the repository's issues URL if you know it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: For keyboard users, there's also a shortcut.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. On GitHub, pressing G and then I jumps to issues. As always, be mindful of where focus is and whether keyboard shortcuts are active for your context.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And once I'm on the issues list page, what should I notice?

speaker: ALEX
text: Notice the page structure. There's typically a heading for the page, controls for filtering, the list of issues, and a New issue button. Each issue entry usually announces its title, number, status, and often metadata like labels or author information. Learning to read this list efficiently matters because you'll use it constantly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's the efficient reading strategy?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use headings or quick navigation where possible. Scan titles first. Then note whether an item is open or closed. Listen for labels that tell you the issue type or priority. And pay attention to who opened it and whether there are comments.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Filtering is probably where lots of people get stuck.

speaker: ALEX
text: It can feel that way at first, but the filter bar is your friend. You can use it to search by keywords and apply structured filters such as labels, milestones, or assignment. Useful queries might include filtering by a label, combining multiple filters, narrowing to a milestone, or searching for a word or phrase from a title or body.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there are also open versus closed filters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Sometimes you want active work. Sometimes you want historical context. Switching between open and closed can tell you whether something is still unresolved or already handled.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the filter buttons can help if you don't want to remember query syntax.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You don't have to start with advanced search strings. Buttons and simple searches are fine.

speaker: ALEX
text: When you open an issue, the first thing is orientation. You've landed on a page with the issue title near the top, its number, status, the original description, then comments and activity below.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So read the description before reacting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Always. Read what happened, where it happens, and what the reporter is asking for. If you're in the terminal, viewing an issue can render Markdown for easier reading. If you need the full web context, you can open the issue in the browser instead. And if you only want the discussion, some tools let you view just the comments.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds especially useful when an issue has a long history.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very useful.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's talk about leaving a comment.

speaker: ALEX
text: A good issue comment is specific and helpful. In the browser, you scroll to the comment box, type your message, and submit it with the Comment button. In many contexts, Ctrl+Enter also submits. If you're using the GitHub CLI, you may write a comment interactively in your default editor, or provide the comment inline on the command line.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Interactive versus inline.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Interactive is nice when the comment is longer and you want to compose carefully in your editor. Inline is convenient for short comments.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And Markdown works while typing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You can use Markdown formatting in issue comments and bodies, which is helpful for steps, code, emphasis, and links.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There were also GitHub shortcuts for issue pages.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, both on the issues list and on an open issue. The exact shortcuts are less important than the habit: learn the navigation aids that match your workflow and accessibility needs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now to the central skill: filing a new issue. In the browser, you navigate to the Issues tab and activate New issue. Some repositories show a template picker. If so, choose a suitable template, or open a blank issue when that's the right fit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And in the terminal?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can create a new issue interactively, where the CLI prompts for title, body, labels, and assignees. Or inline, where you supply everything on the command line. If the repository has templates, use them when they fit the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's slow down on the anatomy of the issue itself.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good idea. The title should be specific. Not vague. Not just "Bug" or "Fix TODO." Someone scanning the list should understand the topic without opening the issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So instead of "Fix TODO," say something like "Fix missing workshop description in welcome.md."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Or "welcome.md TODO placeholder needs real content." Or "Replace TODO in welcome.md intro section with actual welcome text." Those are concrete and useful.

speaker: ALEX
text: For the body, include enough context that another person could find and understand the problem from your description alone. A common structure for a bug-style issue includes what happened, what you expected, how to reproduce it, environment details if relevant, and any additional context.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's unpack those.

speaker: ALEX
text: What happened means the current problem. What I expected means the intended behavior or content. How to reproduce means the steps another person can follow to experience or verify the issue. Environment can include browser, screen reader, operating system, version, or other setup details. Additional context is anything else that helps, like related files, screenshots if appropriate, or impact on users.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for documentation issues, the environment might matter less, but location matters a lot.

speaker: ALEX
text: Precisely. For a docs issue, file name and section are often the crucial details. If a line number is relevant, include it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The challenge gives a very specific task, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In the workshop challenge, you open docs slash welcome dot md and look for a line containing TODO. That TODO marks something needing attention. Then you go to the Issues tab, choose New issue, and file a well-structured issue about what you found.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the description should explain what needs to change, where the problem is, and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Not just "there's a TODO," but what the TODO is pointing to and why replacing it would improve the project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also an evidence step.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You're expected to paste the URL of the issue you created and explain what TODO you found and how you described it. That evidence lives in your assigned challenge issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if someone can't find the TODO?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use Find in the browser or editor and search for the word TODO inside docs slash welcome dot md. If you're unsure what to write in the description, explain the problem as if the reader has never seen the file. If the New issue button is missing, confirm you're actually on the Issues tab, and if issues are disabled, ask the facilitator.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also a peer simulation step.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. You may be asked to open a peer simulation issue, something like a welcome link needing context, and leave a comment about whether the title is clear and whether the needed fix is understandable from the title alone. If your facilitator provides a real buddy repository, you may review your buddy's issue instead.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a smart exercise because it trains you to evaluate issue quality, not just produce one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: The reference solution gives two valid styles. One is bug-report style. For example, a title like "TODO in welcome.md: missing workshop schedule link." The body explains that in docs slash welcome dot md, on a certain line, there's a TODO saying to add a link to the workshop schedule, and that the placeholder should be replaced with an actual link. Then it lists steps to find it: open the file, search for TODO, and locate the comment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's very concrete.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. The second style is feature-request style. For example, "Add accessibility tips section to welcome.md." The body explains that the welcome document covers what students will do but does not mention accessibility features, and suggests adding a short section about screen reader shortcuts and keyboard navigation, perhaps after the "What you will learn" section.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So both a bug report and a feature request can satisfy the challenge, as long as the issue is clear.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That's one of the key lessons. Alternate approaches are acceptable if a stranger could act on what you wrote.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we name what makes a good issue in one quick list?

speaker: ALEX
text: Certainly. Clear title. Enough context. Reproducible location. And I'd add actionable intent: the reader should understand what sort of fix or discussion is needed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That connects nicely to the chapter's agentic strategy idea.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. A clear issue for a human is also a strong prompt for an AI agent. If the issue describes the problem, location, and desired fix, it becomes much easier for a person or a tool to help.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's bring in accessibility-specific issue writing. If you're filing an accessibility issue, be especially concrete. Describe the barrier, who it affects, how to reproduce it, and what assistive technology or browser combination you used if relevant.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So not just "screen reader problem."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Better would be something like: when navigating a specific page or component with a certain screen reader and browser, a button is announced ambiguously, focus order is confusing, a landmark is missing, or a heading hierarchy breaks navigation. Then include what you expected instead.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the same body structure still applies: what happened, what I expected, how to reproduce, environment, additional context.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Accessibility issues are strongest when they're testable and respectful. You're documenting impact, not just frustration.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about cross-referencing issues?

speaker: ALEX
text: Very useful skill. You can mention another issue in a comment to connect related work. You can reference issues across repositories if needed. And in pull request descriptions or even issue comments, closing keywords can automatically close an issue when a related change is merged, depending on context.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So issue numbers are not just labels. They're links in a work graph.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a great way to say it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Sub-issues are another structural tool. If a parent issue is too broad, you can create child issues for each smaller task. The parent can then show progress as sub-issues are completed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When should I use a sub-issue instead of a task list?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use a sub-issue when each step deserves its own title, description, conversation, or tracking. Use a task list when the checklist items are smaller and don't need separate issue-level management.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And how do you create a sub-issue?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open the parent issue, locate the Sub-issues area or related control, choose to add or create a sub-issue, give it a clear title and a short description, and create it. Then the child appears under the parent, and the child issue also shows its parent relationship.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So you can read sub-issues from the parent, and read the parent from the child.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That two-way context is part of what makes them useful.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's shift from contributors to maintainers and triagers.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good. Maintainers and triagers often close issues, reopen them, assign them, add or remove labels, set milestones, and sometimes transfer or delete issues. Closing can be a plain close, a close with a reason, or a close with a comment. Reopening restores discussion when the work isn't actually finished or the issue needs renewed attention.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Assignment matters too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You may assign yourself, assign a teammate if appropriate, add labels, remove labels, or set a milestone to group work into a release or time frame.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And all of this metadata changes how discoverable and manageable the issue is.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Good metadata is part of accessibility too, in a broad sense. It helps people find the right work and understand its state.

speaker: ALEX
text: One label worth calling out for newcomers is good first issue. That label often signals a beginner-friendly entry point into a project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I'm looking for a first contribution, filtering by that label is a smart move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very smart. It gives you issues that maintainers have already identified as approachable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about the local Git alternative mentioned in the chapter?

speaker: ALEX
text: That reminder is important: even if you work from a local clone for code, issue work itself still lives on GitHub. You may inspect files locally to gather context, but the issue is filed and discussed in GitHub's issue system. So local Git can support issue writing, but it doesn't replace GitHub issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That makes sense. Git tracks file history. GitHub issues track the conversation around work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: For blind users, a few practical notes from the workflow. On the issues page, the first heading can confirm you've landed where you intended. Jumping by heading level can speed movement between issue titles. If a template picker appears, tabbing through and listening for link text like "Open a blank issue" can help.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for low-vision users?

speaker: ALEX
text: The New issue button is typically prominent and remains visible at high zoom. The title field is wide, the body field expands as you type, and after submission your new issue page shows the title and description in a layout that's usually easy to verify. Check that the rendered content says what you meant.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Sighted users also get visual confirmation from the green Open badge and the issue appearing in the list.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And everyone should notice the auto-assigned issue number in the page title and URL, because that number becomes useful for references later.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's walk through the chapter's two main challenges cleanly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Challenge two: create your first issue. Open your Learning Room repository. Go to Issues. Choose New issue. If there's a template picker, use a fitting template or open a blank issue. Write a clear title, ideally at least a dozen characters and genuinely descriptive. Write a meaningful body, ideally with enough detail that another person can act. Include what the problem is, where it appears in the repository, and what you think the fix should be. Submit the issue. Then copy the issue URL or note the issue number.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And how do I know I'm done?

speaker: ALEX
text: Your issue appears in the issues list, your username is the author, the title is clear, and the description is detailed enough to understand the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Challenge three?

speaker: ALEX
text: Find another student's issue or a facilitator-provided peer simulation issue. Open it. Read the description carefully. Go to the comment box. Write a helpful comment and include an @mention of the issue author by username. Then submit it. You're done when the comment appears and the mention renders as a clickable username link.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the reason @mentions matter is notifications.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. They notify the specific person who should see the comment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there's the optional extension.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Add a sub-issue to a larger issue if sub-issues are enabled in your repository and you have permission. A good sub-issue title names one specific piece of the larger problem. For example, instead of one broad "Fix accessibility in welcome.md," you might create sub-issues for alt text, heading hierarchy, or link clarity.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And after all that, there's still one administrative step.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Post evidence in your assigned challenge issue. A simple completion comment can list the issue number you created, the issue where you commented with an @mention, and optionally the sub-issue you added. Then close your assigned challenge issue if the workshop instructions tell you to do that. The facilitator reviews your activity directly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So completion is visible and checkable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, which makes the learning process fair and transparent.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There was also a broader teaching point here: file, read, comment on, and triage real issues manually before relying on agents.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's an excellent closing lesson. If you can't evaluate issue quality yourself, you can't judge whether an AI assistant is helping well. Manual skill comes first. Then tools can amplify your judgment rather than replace it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So today, we learn to be the triage engine.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And once you understand issue quality, metadata, references, and communication, you're ready for more advanced workflows later.

speaker: ALEX
text: One final note on sources. When you need the current source of truth, use GitHub Docs, the GitHub Changelog, and GitHub's issue documentation. For accessibility-specific workflows, GitHub's accessibility guide for issues and the CLI accessibility guide are especially valuable companions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a good habit: learn the workflow here, then verify evolving details from the official docs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And for now, your next move is simple: find a TODO, write a clear issue, and make it easy for the next person to help.
